text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CO-MANAGEMENT VISIT
18-Month Post-Treatment Surveillance Note

ATTENDING PHYSICIANS: Head and Neck Oncology / Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 18-month post-chemoradiation surveillance visit for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 54-year-old never-smoking Nigerian-American woman with a history of p16-positive (HPV RNA ISH-confirmed) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, originally staged T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition Stage I, HPV-positive oropharynx classification), who presents today for her 18-month post-treatment surveillance visit.

To briefly review her history: she initially presented approximately two years ago with left-sided throat discomfort, globus sensation, and left-sided otalgia. Workup at that time revealed a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass with a left level IIA necrotic lymph node (2.7 cm). Biopsy of the base of tongue confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong, diffuse p16 positivity and HPV RNA ISH positivity. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node confirmed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. Staging PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease only -- intensely hypermetabolic primary (SUVmax 14.2) and hypermetabolic left IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) with no distant disease. Tumor board consensus favored definitive concurrent chemoradiation; she was not eligible for available de-escalation trials due to the necrotic lymphadenopathy exclusion criterion.

She completed definitive IMRT (70 Gy to the high-risk planning target volume in 35 fractions via SIB-VMAT technique) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22, and a dose-reduced third cycle at 75 mg/m2 on day 43 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 3 mucositis. She required hospitalization during week 4 of treatment for confluent grade 3 oropharyngeal mucositis with complete inability to tolerate oral intake, necessitating IV hydration, IV PCA morphine, and escalation to full enteral nutrition through her prophylactic PEG tube. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis, mild oral candidiasis, hypomagnesemia requiring repletion, and notable fatigue. She completed all 35 fractions without treatment interruption.

Post-treatment PET-CT at approximately 10 weeks showed a complete metabolic response -- the primary site and the previously involved IIA node were both without FDG-avid activity. Flexible laryngoscopy at that time was reassuring with healing mucosa and no gross residual tumor. Her PEG tube was removed at approximately 3 months post-treatment once she had re-established adequate oral intake.

Subsequent surveillance CT of the neck and chest at 6 months and 12 months post-treatment both showed no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastatic disease. At the 12-month visit, modified barium swallow confirmed mild pharyngeal dysmotility without aspiration. She was also noted at the 8-month mark to have developed grade 2 late radiation-related cervical fibrosis treated with physical therapy, and radiation-induced hypothyroidism requiring an increase in her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Most recent 18-month surveillance CT of the neck (obtained in advance of today's visit) showed no evidence of recurrence, with a stable, small fibrotic remnant in the left IIA nodal region.

Today, Ms. Okonkwo reports that she is doing extremely well overall and is pleased with her recovery trajectory. She has returned to full-time employment as a high school English teacher, which she describes as her passion, and is managing well in that role. She denies any throat pain, odynophagia, new or worsening neck swelling, otalgia, weight loss, hemoptysis, hoarseness, trismus, or referred ear pain. She reports that her swallowing has continued to improve since her last visit and that she is tolerating most foods. She does note persistent but mild xerostomia that is stable and manageable. She uses biotene spray and xylitol lozenges, which she finds helpful. She denies any new lumps or bumps in the neck. She has no respiratory symptoms. No constitutional complaints. She is engaged in a survivorship-oriented exercise program and is inquiring about a support group for patients with HPV-positive head and neck cancer, which we will facilitate.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Energy level is good.
HEENT: Mild xerostomia as noted above, grade 1 by her report. No epistaxis. No significant trismus. Mild baseline dysphagia to certain textures (described further below). No new neck masses or tenderness. No ear pain at this time.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath, cough, or hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite good. Tolerating diet well. Regular bowel habits. PEG removed approximately 15 months ago without complication.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria.
Musculoskeletal: Some residual left neck stiffness, improving with physical therapy. No new joint complaints.
Neurological: No headaches, focal neurological deficits, or paresthesias.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism followed and managed (see below).
Psychiatric: She reports a positive outlook. Mentions mild situational anxiety around surveillance visits, which she manages well. Denies depression.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 100 mcg orally once daily (increased from 75 mcg approximately 10 months ago for radiation-induced hypothyroidism)
2. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (for GERD)
3. Biotene oral rinse / spray as needed for xerostomia
4. Xylitol lozenges as needed
5. Multivitamin once daily
6. Supplemental fluoride gel (custom dental trays, nightly) -- radiation caries prophylaxis per dental oncology recommendation

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (for reference in co-management context):
- HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (as above) -- treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation, currently in surveillance
- Well-controlled hypothyroidism (baseline, predating cancer diagnosis; worsened following radiation, now on higher dose levothyroxine)
- Mild GERD on PPI

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- High school English teacher, fully employed, full-time
- Never-smoker
- No significant alcohol use
- Nigerian-American descent
- Lives with spouse; strong support system
- No known exposure to environmental carcinogens

FAMILY HISTORY: No family history of head and neck cancers, oropharyngeal malignancy, or HPV-associated cancers per prior documentation.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm and regular, temperature 36.8 C, weight 66.8 kg (up from 63 kg at end of treatment nadir, approaching pre-treatment baseline of 68 kg), BMI 23.7.

General: Ms. Okonkwo is a well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. She is alert and conversational, with normal prosody and intelligible speech. Affect is pleasant.

Head: Normocephalic. No scalp lesions.

Eyes: Conjunctivae clear. Sclerae anicteric.

Ears: External ears and canals without lesions or discharge. No pre- or post-auricular lymphadenopathy.

Nose/Sinuses: No nasal lesions. Nontender to palpation over sinuses.

Oral Cavity: Mucosa moist but with reduced salivary pooling consistent with chronic xerostomia. No oral ulceration, leukoplakia, erythema, or suspicious mucosal lesions. Tongue protrudes midline without fasciculations. Dentition intact with ongoing fluoride treatment; no carious lesions appreciated on this exam. Soft palate rises symmetrically.

Oropharynx / Flexible Laryngoscopy (performed in clinic today): Base of tongue mucosa is smooth and intact without ulceration, exophytic lesion, or mucosal irregularity. No asymmetric fullness or mass effect at the base of tongue. Bilateral valleculae clear. Epiglottis smooth without edema. Supraglottic and glottic larynx visualized -- true and false vocal folds are symmetric and mobile bilaterally without lesions. Hypopharynx is unremarkable. No pooling of secretions. Findings are reassuring for no gross mucosal recurrence.

Neck: Mild left-sided soft tissue induration in the left submandibular and anterior neck region consistent with known late radiation fibrosis, stable in character compared to prior examinations. No new or enlarging lymph nodes palpated. No palpable lymphadenopathy in the left or right level I, II, III, IV, or V chains. No palpable supraclavicular or posterior cervical adenopathy. Thyroid gland is without palpable nodules.

Chest/Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Breath sounds clear bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. No organomegaly. PEG tube site: well-healed without erythema or granulation tissue.

Extremities: No edema.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Labs drawn at this visit (and/or within the past 2 weeks):
- CBC: WBC 6.2 x10^9/L (normal differential), Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Hct 39.2%, Plt 238 x10^9/L -- all within normal limits
- Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 103, CO2 25, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (baseline recovered from treatment-related nephrotoxicity), eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2, Glucose 91, ALT 22, AST 19, Alk Phos 68, Total Bilirubin 0.6, Albumin 4.2 -- all within normal limits
- TSH: 2.3 mIU/L (within normal range; patient maintained on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily)
- Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL (normal; was previously hypomagnesemic during cisplatin treatment, now normalized)

Imaging (reviewed prior to this visit):
- CT neck with contrast obtained approximately 2 weeks ago (18-month surveillance imaging): No evidence of local recurrence at the primary base of tongue site. Stable small fibrotic remnant in the left level IIA nodal region, reduced and non-suspicious. No new cervical, retropharyngeal, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Limited chest survey without pulmonary nodules or masses. Overall reassuring findings with no radiographic evidence of disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 -- 18-MONTH POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE

Ms. Okonkwo continues to demonstrate an excellent and durable response to treatment. At 18 months following completion of definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT, she has no clinical or radiographic evidence of local, regional, or distant recurrence. The primary base of tongue site appears entirely healed on endoscopy. The previously involved left IIA node has regressed dramatically from 2.7 cm to a small non-suspicious fibrotic remnant on serial imaging. This trajectory is entirely consistent with what we expect in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer with an excellent initial complete metabolic response, particularly in a never-smoking patient.

We reviewed with Ms. Okonkwo the current literature landscape around surveillance in this setting. We noted that per emerging data, including the pooled analysis from several cooperative group trials and retrospective institutional series, the recurrence risk for patients with HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer after achieving complete response continues to decrease with time. The vast majority of recurrences, when they do occur, happen within the first 2 years. At 18 months post-treatment without any evidence of recurrence, her likelihood of achieving durable cure is very high. We discussed the current ASCO head and neck cancer surveillance guidelines, which support clinical examination as the primary tool, with imaging used selectively based on clinical suspicion rather than on a rigid fixed schedule for patients with a documented complete metabolic response.

We also briefly reviewed the de-escalation trial literature at this visit given her ongoing interest -- including ORATOR2, NRG-HN002, and the PATHOS trial framework. While these are relevant to newly diagnosed patients being considered for treatment intensity reduction, they inform our understanding of the favorable biology in HPV-positive, never-smoking patients like Ms. Okonkwo, reinforcing that she received appropriate standard-of-care treatment and has every reason to expect long-term disease-free survival.

Surveillance Plan: We will continue surveillance at 6-month intervals through years 2 to 3 post-treatment, then extend to annual visits thereafter. The next visit will be in approximately 6 months (the 24-month mark). We will plan a CT neck at that time as part of her 2-year surveillance. PET-CT is not routinely planned given the durable complete metabolic response already documented -- imaging will be driven by clinical findings going forward.

2. LATE RADIATION TOXICITIES -- ONGOING MANAGEMENT

a. Xerostomia (grade 1): Stable and subjectively mild. Patient continues biotene spray, xylitol lozenges, and adequate hydration. She has adapted her diet and teaching practices (carries water bottle to class). We do not anticipate significant further improvement at this stage; salivary gland function is largely at its nadir by 12-18 months post-radiation. We will refer to dental oncology for ongoing fluoride treatment and monitoring for late radiation-induced caries, which can present insidiously in this population.

b. Dysphagia (mild, grade 1): Patient reports near-normal swallowing function with only mild difficulty with hard, dry textures. She continues to apply chin-tuck maneuver when needed (learned through speech-language pathology). We will check in on this at subsequent visits; if there is any new or worsening swallowing difficulty, we will refer back to speech-language pathology for repeat modified barium swallow study and potential additional dysphagia rehabilitation.

c. Cervical radiation fibrosis (grade 2, improving): Left neck induration is stable to mildly improved. She has been engaged with physical therapy for cervical range-of-motion exercises and myofascial release with perceived benefit. We encouraged her to continue these exercises independently. No new fibrosis-related concerns at this time.

d. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism: Well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH 2.3 today, which is in range. We will continue monitoring TSH at routine surveillance intervals (every 6-12 months). No dose adjustment needed at this time.

e. Radiation caries prevention: Patient compliant with nightly fluoride gel application using custom trays as prescribed by dental oncology. She is scheduled for dental follow-up in 2 months. We will continue to coordinate with dental oncology.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM (COMBINED PRE-EXISTING AND RADIATION-INDUCED)
As above, well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH within normal limits today.

4. GERD
No active symptoms today. Continues omeprazole 20 mg daily.

5. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
Ms. Okonkwo expressed great interest in connecting with an HPV-positive head and neck cancer survivorship network and support group. We will provide a referral to our institution's cancer survivorship program and share information about the SPOHNC (Support for People with Oral and Head and Neck Cancer) network. We discussed the importance of continued sun protection, dental surveillance, and thyroid monitoring as long-term survivorship priorities. We also acknowledged that surveillance visit anxiety ("scanxiety") is a common and normal experience and provided reassurance. Psychological support resources were offered if needed.

6. DISPOSITION
Ms. Okonkwo was seen today in joint head and neck oncology and medical oncology co-management. She is in excellent general health with a durable complete response at 18 months post-treatment. No treatment is planned or needed at this time. She will follow up in 6 months for her 24-month surveillance visit with CT neck imaging.

Return precautions discussed: She was advised to call or present earlier than scheduled if she develops any new neck swelling, a new palpable lump, throat pain, odynophagia, otalgia, unexplained weight loss, or any other new concerning symptoms.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 40 minutes.

Electronically signed by Attending, Head and Neck Oncology / Medical Oncology
